questions.q: What is SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) and what is its primary function?
options: SMTP is used to send (submit) emails from a client to a mail server or between mail servers; it uses TCP port 25 (unauthenticated), 587 (authenticated), or 465 (TLS), and is not used for retrieving email
SMTP is used to retrieve emails from a mail server to a client
SMTP is a protocol for transferring files over the internet, similar to HTTP
SMTP handles both sending and retrieving emails, eliminating the need for separate protocols
questions.explanation: SMTP handles outbound mail. A client connects to an SMTP server (using AUTH for authentication), submits the message, and the server relays it to the recipient's mail server. Retrieval uses POP3 or IMAP. Port 25 is for server-to-server; 587 (submission) requires authentication; 465 adds TLS. SMTP is push-only; retrieval is separate.

questions.q: What are the main differences between POP3 and IMAP for email retrieval?
options: POP3 downloads emails to the client and deletes them from the server (stateless); IMAP keeps emails on the server and allows synchronization of folder structures, flags, and multiple clients accessing the same mailbox
POP3 and IMAP are identical protocols with interchangeable names
IMAP is faster because it uses a binary format while POP3 uses text
POP3 supports modern features like folder management; IMAP does not
questions.explanation: POP3 is simple: connect, download, disconnect. Emails are typically deleted from the server after download, so only one client sees them. IMAP is stateful: flags (read, flagged), folders, and syncing are preserved on the server. Multiple clients can access the same mailbox. POP3 uses port 110 (or 995 TLS); IMAP uses 143 (or 993 TLS).

questions.q: What is FTP (File Transfer Protocol) and how does it differ from HTTP for file transfer?
options: FTP uses a separate control connection (port 21) and data connection (port 20 active or dynamic passive) for reliability and resume capability; HTTP embeds file transfer in a single request-response, making it stateless but less efficient for large files or restarts
FTP and HTTP are identical; the choice is purely aesthetic
HTTP is more secure because FTP sends credentials in plaintext; FTP is therefore deprecated everywhere
FTP only transfers binary files; HTTP only transfers text
questions.explanation: FTP maintains a stateful session with separate channels: control (commands, credentials) and data (file content). This enables mid-transfer resume, directory listing, and append operations. HTTP uses single stateless requests; resumption requires Range headers. FTP credentials are plaintext (use SFTP or FTPS for security). HTTP is simpler and firewalls-friendly; FTP requires port 20/21 access.

questions.q: What is WebSocket and how does it enable real-time bidirectional communication?
options: WebSocket establishes a persistent TCP connection with HTTP upgrade, supporting low-latency, full-duplex messages (client ↔ server simultaneously) without polling; ideal for chat, gaming, live updates
WebSocket is a variant of HTTP that fetches multiple resources in parallel
WebSocket only supports unidirectional communication (client to server)
WebSocket eliminates the need for TCP, using UDP for lower latency
questions.explanation: WebSocket (RFC 6455) initiates with HTTP Upgrade request; once upgraded, TCP carries framed messages in both directions without HTTP overhead. No polling required; server can push updates immediately. Full-duplex enables real-time applications (chat, collaborative editing, live feeds). UDP version does not exist; WebSocket requires TCP. HTTP itself is request-response (half-duplex per cycle).

questions.q: What is gRPC and what advantages does it offer compared to REST?
options: gRPC uses Protocol Buffers for serialization and HTTP/2 multiplexing, enabling low-latency, strongly-typed, bidirectional streaming APIs; REST uses JSON and HTTP/1.1, often requiring polling or WebSockets for streaming
gRPC is a slower version of REST used for legacy systems
gRPC only works for cloud services; REST works everywhere
REST and gRPC are identical; gRPC is just REST with authentication
questions.explanation: gRPC (Google RPC) uses Protocol Buffers (efficient binary serialization) and HTTP/2 (multiplexing, server push). Define services in .proto; code generation creates strongly-typed clients and servers. REST uses JSON (verbose) and HTTP/1.1 (serial requests). gRPC supports streaming (client, server, bidirectional) natively. gRPC is faster and more efficient; REST is more universal and human-readable.

questions.q: What is the MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) protocol and what are its typical use cases?
options: MQTT is a lightweight publish-subscribe protocol designed for IoT and bandwidth-constrained environments; uses broker (message broker) to decouple publishers and subscribers; supports QoS levels 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once)
MQTT is similar to HTTP and is used primarily for web services
MQTT requires a dedicated TCP port 8883 and does not support any other port
MQTT guarantees exactly-once delivery for all messages
questions.explanation: MQTT is ideal for resource-constrained IoT devices: small protocol overhead (2-byte header), binary payload, optional persistent connection. Publish-subscribe decouples devices; broker stores subscriptions. QoS 0 is fire-and-forget; QoS 1 retries until ACK; QoS 2 prevents duplicates (expensive). Default port is 1883 (TLS: 8883). Not all messages require QoS 2; choose per message.

questions.q: What is CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) and how does it differ from MQTT for IoT?
options: CoAP uses UDP (connectionless, low overhead) with request-response similar to HTTP/REST; MQTT uses TCP (connection-oriented) with publish-subscribe; CoAP is lighter for simple sensors, MQTT for complex messaging topologies
CoAP is identical to MQTT with a different name
CoAP requires a permanent connection; MQTT is connectionless
MQTT is for IoT; CoAP is only for web browsers
questions.explanation: CoAP (RFC 7252) is UDP-based (no connection overhead), making it extremely lightweight for simple devices (sensors, actuators). Offers GET/POST/PUT/DELETE like REST. MQTT (TCP) is better for complex message routing, bridging devices, and reliable delivery across unstable networks. CoAP: simple, stateless. MQTT: complex, stateful. Choose based on device capability and use case.

questions.q: What are the key characteristics of DNS (Domain Name System) as an application-layer protocol?
options: DNS uses UDP port 53 for queries (fast) and TCP 53 for zone transfers; hierarchical, distributed database translates domain names to IP addresses; supports caching and recursive/iterative resolution
DNS uses only TCP and is deprecated in favor of direct IP addressing
DNS returns only IPv4 addresses; IPv6 requires a separate protocol
DNS queries always traverse all root nameservers to ensure correctness
questions.explanation: DNS is hierarchical: client queries resolver → root → TLD → authoritative nameserver. UDP is fast for typical queries; TCP handles large responses (zone transfers, DNSSEC). Caching at each resolver reduces latency and load. Recursive resolution (resolver queries on behalf of client) is common. DNS also handles IPv6 (AAAA records), MX (mail), TXT records, etc. Not all queries touch root servers due to caching.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the Nagle algorithm in TCP and when might disabling it be beneficial?
options: The Nagle algorithm batches small packets, waiting for ACK before sending more small data, reducing overhead but increasing latency; disabled (TCP_NODELAY) for real-time interactive applications like games, VoIP, or SSH where latency matters more than efficiency
The Nagle algorithm guarantees all packets arrive without loss
Disabling Nagle increases security by encrypting data
The Nagle algorithm only affects IPv6, not IPv4
questions.explanation: Nagle prevents slow-start syndrome when applications send small bursts (e.g., each keystroke). By batching, TCP avoids sending many small packets with large headers. But batching adds latency (wait for ACK). Interactive applications disable TCP_NODELAY to send immediately. Long-lived bulk transfers keep Nagle enabled for efficiency. Nagle affects both IPv4 and IPv6.

questions.q: What is AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol) and how does it support reliable message delivery?
options: AMQP is a message-oriented middleware protocol with guaranteed delivery via publisher acknowledgments and consumer acknowledgments; supports exchanges, queues, and routing; used by RabbitMQ, enabling persistence and transaction support for reliable distributed systems
AMQP is identical to MQTT and is used for simple sensor networks
AMQP only supports message publishing; subscribers cannot confirm receipt
AMQP guarantees zero latency by eliminating acknowledgment overhead
questions.explanation: AMQP (1.0 standard) is more sophisticated than MQTT: publishers get confirmation messages were queued; consumers confirm receipt. Exchanges route messages to queues by rules. Persistence stores messages on disk. Transactions group operations atomically. RabbitMQ implements AMQP; suitable for financial systems, critical messaging. MQTT is simpler and lighter; AMQP is more reliable but heavier. ACKs add latency but ensure delivery.
